3: Ammonia (NH3) plays an important role in nitrogen (N) cycling (Erisman et al., 2007; Galloway et al., 2003; Ti et al., 2019), and NH3 emitted into the atmosphere affects human health through the

formation of aerosols (Erisman and Schaap, 2004; McCubbin et al., 2002). Brink et al. (2011) reported that the health cost caused by NH3 emission in the European Union was 18-140 billion US$ yr-1. Moreover, NH3 in the atmosphere or deposited to the earth's sur- face can degrade air quality, and cause soil acidification, eutrophi- cation and biodiversity loss (Bergstrom and Janssom, 2006; Makell et al., 2010; Stevens et al., 2011). NH3, nitrous oxide (N20) and methane (CH4) are recognized as direct or indirect greenhouse gases (GHGs) that can cause other environmental problems, e.g. global warming, ozone damage and photochemical smog (Stolarski et al., 2015; Tingas et al., 2016).

China is a major contributor to global NH3 and GHGs emissions.

Agricultural NH3 and GHG emissions from China were estimated at 10.6 Tg NH3 in 2008 (Zhang et al., 2018) and 1786 Tg CO2 equivalent in 2016 (Wu et al., 2019), respectively, and dairy production is a significant source. Dairy production in China includes systems with outdoor grazing of forages through to cow housing with concen- trated feeding(Zhang et al., 2017), while NH3 and GHG emissions can occur from all stages of the dairy production system, including manure deposited onto pasture, on the floor of dairy buildings, manure storage and applied to farmland (Bai et al., 2016; Dämmgen and Hutchings, 2008; Webb and Misselbrook, 2004; Zhang et al., 2017). As dairy production in China is shifting from traditional and grazing systems to industrialized concentrated animal feeding systems, NH3 and GHG emissions from industrialized systems have become a hot-spot requiring mitigation (Zhang et al., 2019).

Several previous studies have summarized NH3 emission abatement measures at various manure management stages, which mainly include three basic approaches: (1) diet manipulation, (2) manure treatment, and (3) capture of emitted gases (Hou et al., 2015; Hristov et al., 2011). In addition, the link between crop- livestock production and reduction of fertilizer use in crop/feed production is another effective approach to mitigate NH3 emission. It has been suggested that the mitigation measures with great potential for NH3 emission mitigation are acidification and coverage of manure during manure storage (Hou et al., 2015). Zhang et al. (2019) also noted that acidification of the manure surface and a vermiculite cover showed high NH3 emission miti- gation potential. However, a technology to mitigate one source of the emission may affect the other sources. Berg et al. (2006) found acidification with lactic acid had great potential for reduction of NH3, N20 and CH4 emissions. In contrast, VanderZaag et al. (2009) found that a straw cover reduced NH3 emission from liquid dairy manure but increased N20 emission. Additionally, a technology to mitigate one source of the emission may also affect downstream emissions in the manure management chain (Kai et al., 2008; Fangueiro et al., 2014). Zhang et al. (2019) estimated the impacts of mitigation technologies on down-stream emissions based on a modelling approach. However, few studies (Kai et al., 2008) have accounted for multiple gaseous emissions, cumulative effects of emissions at various stages based on trial data, or emissions from a whole farm system.

Reactive nitrogen footprint (NF) and carbon footprint (CF) refer to the sum of all reactive N or GHGs emissions, respectively, on a functional unit of product or service basis (Leach et al., 2012; Ledgard et al., 2011). Life cycle assessment (LCA) is a widely used method, which provides a comprehensive perspective to include all sources of N and GHGs emissions covering production of a product, as well as all the background processes, and reveal the hotspots of environmental impacts through the whole supply chain (Finnveden et al., 2009). There have been several LCA-based studies focusing on NF and CF of livestock production. Ledgard et al. (2019) found higher reactive NF and CF from housed-cow systems in China than that from pasture-based systems in New Zealand and NH3 and CH4 emissions were the main contributor to NF and CF, respectively. Adom et al. (2012) compared CF among different dairy feeds for milk production and found application of inorganic N fertilizer was the main contributor to the CF of dairy feed production for most regions in the USA. Thus, LCA is a useful method to comprehen- sively evaluate the combined environmental effects of mitigation technologies used on farm.

In the present study, we used an LCA approach linked with in- cubation studies to: i) determine efficiency of the ammonia miti- gation measures (i.e. acidifying the manure surface under a slatted floor during the housing stage and covering manure with vermic- ulite during the storage stage) on NH3, N20 and CH4 emissions from the "housing-storage" manure management chain, ii) analyze

effects of the ammonia mitigation measures on the NF of milk production from the "cradle-to-farm gate" of a typical housed cow dairy system, and iii) explore the influence of ammonia mitigation measures on the CF of milk production.

Dairy production in China includes systems with outdoor grazing of forages through to cow housing with concen- trated feeding(Zhang et al., 2017), while NH3 and GHG emissions can occur from all stages of the dairy production system, including manure deposited onto pasture, on the floor of dairy buildings, manure storage and applied to farmland (Bai et al., 2016; Dämmgen and Hutchings, 2008; Webb and Misselbrook, 2004; Zhang et al., 2017). As dairy production in China is shifting from traditional and grazing systems to industrialized concentrated animal feeding systems, NH3 and GHG emissions from industrialized systems have become a hot-spot requiring mitigation (Zhang et al., 2019).

Several previous studies have summarized NH3 emission abatement measures at various manure management stages, which mainly include three basic approaches: (1) diet manipulation, (2) manure treatment, and (3) capture of emitted gases (Hou et al., 2015; Hristov et al., 2011). In addition, the link between crop- livestock production and reduction of fertilizer use in crop/feed production is another effective approach to mitigate NH3 emission. It has been suggested that the mitigation measures with great potential for NH3 emission mitigation are acidification and coverage of manure during manure storage (Hou et al., 2015). Zhang et al. (2019) also noted that acidification of the manure surface and a vermiculite cover showed high NH3 emission miti- gation potential. However, a technology to mitigate one source of the emission may affect the other sources. Berg et al. (2006) found acidification with lactic acid had great potential for reduction of NH3, N20 and CH4 emissions. In contrast, VanderZaag et al. (2009) found that a straw cover reduced NH3 emission from liquid dairy manure but increased N20 emission. Additionally, a technology to mitigate one source of the emission may also affect downstream emissions in the manure management chain (Kai et al., 2008; Fangueiro et al., 2014). Zhang et al. (2019) estimated the impacts of mitigation technologies on down-stream emissions based on a modelling approach. However, few studies (Kai et al., 2008) have accounted for multiple gaseous emissions, cumulative effects of emissions at various stages based on trial data, or emissions from a whole farm system.

Reactive nitrogen footprint (NF) and carbon footprint (CF) refer to the sum of all reactive N or GHGs emissions, respectively, on a functional unit of product or service basis (Leach et al., 2012; Ledgard et al., 2011). Life cycle assessment (LCA) is a widely used method, which provides a comprehensive perspective to include all sources of N and GHGs emissions covering production of a product, as well as all the background processes, and reveal the hotspots of environmental impacts through the whole supply chain (Finnveden et al., 2009). There have been several LCA-based studies focusing on NF and CF of livestock production. Ledgard et al. (2019) found higher reactive NF and CF from housed-cow systems in China than that from pasture-based systems in New Zealand and NH3 and CH4 emissions were the main contributor to NF and CF, respectively. Adom et al. (2012) compared CF among different dairy feeds for milk production and found application of inorganic N fertilizer was the main contributor to the CF of dairy feed production for most regions in the USA. Thus, LCA is a useful method to comprehen- sively evaluate the combined environmental effects of mitigation technologies used on farm.

In the present study, we used an LCA approach linked with in- cubation studies to: i) determine efficiency of the ammonia miti- gation measures (i.e. acidifying the manure surface under a slatted floor during the housing stage and covering manure with vermic- ulite during the storage stage) on NH3, N20 and CH4 emissions from the "housing-storage" manure management chain, ii) analyze

effects of the ammonia mitigation measures on the NF of milk production from the "cradle-to-farm gate" of a typical housed cow dairy system, and iii) explore the influence of ammonia mitigation measures on the CF of milk production.
8: A consecutive incubation trial, connecting "housing" and "stor- age" stages on a dairy farm, was conducted to measure effects of selected NH3 mitigation measures (acidification of the manure surface under a slatted floor during animal housing and a vermic- ulite cover on manure during storage) on NH3, N20 and CH4 emissions. Then, data from the incubation trial were used in an LCA to assess the influence of these mitigation measures on the NF and CF of milk for a typical dairy farm system (Fig. 1) based on infor- mation from a literature review and a local survey.
9: <header>2.2. Dairy farm system in north China plain </header>

The North China Plain is the largest milk production region in China (Wang et al., 2018a), and the proportion of milk production in this region to China was nearly 25% in the last few years (Wang et al., 2018b). Dairy production in this region is dominated by an- imal housing systems reliant on brought-in concentrate feeds. A commercial dairy farm in this region was selected as being a typical representative farm system based on previous farm surveys (Lu et al., 2018; Wang et al., 2018a). Details of the cows and feed input in this dairy farm are shown in Table 1, which were derived from local investigation. There were 558 cows with 3.2 kiloton fat- and protein-corrected milk (FPCM; IDF, 2015) produced per year on the farm. Feed ingredients mainly included maize silage, concen- trate feeds, alfalfa and oat hay. The bedding materials used in the dairy housing were sand in cubical areas, and there were no other bedding materials inside the dairy housing. Manure in the dairy building was collected daily using an automatic scraper, then stored in an open tank system for 1-2 months, after which most was applied to nearby orchard or vegetable areas, with the rest dis- charged (nearly 20% of total) directly to water (Wei, 2016). For the analysis in this study we assumed that the dairy building had a slatted floor, and manure under the slatted floor was collected daily from the dairy building and then stored in an outside storage fa- cility for 38 days according to the farmer practice.
10: <header>2.3. Manure collection and storage before the trial started </header>

Manure used in the present study was collected from the floor of the case-study farm mentioned above using shovels and kept in PVC buckets at an average temperature of 4 °C for less than 4 h before the trial started. Basic chemical characteristics of the collected manure are detailed in Table 2.

Details of four treatments in the present study were as follows: 1) manure placed into a vessel under the slatted floor without abatement techniques for 1 day, then stored in a simulated storage facility for 38 days (control), 2) manure placed into a vessel under the slatted floor without abatement techniques for 1 day, then stored in a simulated storage facility with vermiculite covering for 38 days, 3) manure placed into a vessel under the slatted floor was acidified on the surface for 1 day then mixed and stored in a simulated storage facility for 38 days, and 4) manure placed into a vessel under the slatted floor was acidified on the surface for 1 day then mixed and stored in the simulated storage facility with vermiculite covering for 38 days. 2.4.2. Operation of the selected mitigation measures Housing stage: Manure placed in the vessel under the slatted floor was 5 cm thick, representing the thickness of manure under the slatted floor. Surface acidification of manure involved spraying a 3 mm layer of analytical grade 98% sulfuric acid (H2SO4), diluted 100 times with distilled water, which has shown great mitigation

potential with less acid from preliminary trials. The volume of diluted H2SO4 solution used in each treatment was sprayed on the manure surface as evenly as possible. Dilution rate in the present study was the most cost-effective one with least acid input and higher mitigation efficiency based on a pre-trial with several dilution rates and dosages.

Storage stage: After the one-day housing stage, manure was fully mixed then moved to the simulated storage facility and stored for 38 days, representing the average farmer practice. Manure was 25 cm deep in the simulated storage facility with 6 cm vermiculite cover on the manure surface (vermiculite covering treatment). The depth of manure at 25 cm in storage facility was used according to the real situation on the case-study dairy farm. Density and cation exchange capacity of the vermiculite used in the trial were 80 kg m and 110 cmol kg-1 respectively. During the whole trial period, manure stayed at a moderate temperature (15 °C) in a controlled temperature shed that was used to explore the effect of gas emission mitigation effects without the influence of other cli- matic factors.
During the housing stage, NH3 emissions were measured after 0, 6, 12, 18 and 24 h, and the time for each sampling period for NH3

was 60-200 min during the housing stage. During the manure storage stage, NH3 emission was measured after 1, 2, 3, 4, 7, 10, 17, 24 and 38 days, and the time for each sampling period for NH3 lasted for 1-8 h during housing stage. NH3 emissions were measured with a ventilation chamber method using a ventilation rate of 0.2 and 1 times of air exchange per minute in head space for housing and storage stages, respectively, to simulate the real situ- ation referring to the study of Pereira et al. (2011). Ventilation of the chamber was kept outside the sampling time for NH3 and GHGs. N20 and CH4 emissions were measured after 0, 6, 12, 18 and 24 during the housing stage, and after 1, 2, 4, 7, 10, 17, 30, and 38 days during the storage stage, using a static chamber method (Luo et al., 2013). During each measurement, gas samples were collected at 0, 10, 20 and 30 min after covering and sealing the top of the simu- lated facilities for both housing and storage stages and stored in evacuated tedlar gas bags (50 ml), then analyzed using gas chro- matography (Agilent 6820, Agilent, USA). Emission fluxes of N20 and CH4 were integrated over time using a trapezoidal approach to estimate the total emissions over the measurement period.

Surface acidification of manure involved spraying a 3 mm layer of analytical grade 98% sulfuric acid (H2SO4), diluted 100 times with distilled water, which has shown great mitigation

potential with less acid from preliminary trials. The volume of diluted H2SO4 solution used in each treatment was sprayed on the manure surface as evenly as possible. Dilution rate in the present study was the most cost-effective one with least acid input and higher mitigation efficiency based on a pre-trial with several dilution rates and dosages.

Storage stage: After the one-day housing stage, manure was fully mixed then moved to the simulated storage facility and stored for 38 days, representing the average farmer practice. Manure was 25 cm deep in the simulated storage facility with 6 cm vermiculite cover on the manure surface (vermiculite covering treatment). The depth of manure at 25 cm in storage facility was used according to the real situation on the case-study dairy farm. Density and cation exchange capacity of the vermiculite used in the trial were 80 kg m and 110 cmol kg-1 respectively. During the whole trial period, manure stayed at a moderate temperature (15 °C) in a controlled temperature shed that was used to explore the effect of gas emission mitigation effects without the influence of other cli- matic factors.
2.4.3. Gas sampling and emission estimation
During the housing stage, NH3 emissions were measured after 0, 6, 12, 18 and 24 h, and the time for each sampling period for NH3

was 60-200 min during the housing stage. During the manure storage stage, NH3 emission was measured after 1, 2, 3, 4, 7, 10, 17, 24 and 38 days, and the time for each sampling period for NH3 lasted for 1-8 h during housing stage. NH3 emissions were measured with a ventilation chamber method using a ventilation rate of 0.2 and 1 times of air exchange per minute in head space for housing and storage stages, respectively, to simulate the real situ- ation referring to the study of Pereira et al. (2011). Ventilation of the chamber was kept outside the sampling time for NH3 and GHGs. 22: <header>2.5.1. System boundary and general description of life cycle assessment methodology </header>

In this study, the system boundary was from the cradle to farm gate (Fig. 1). It accounted for feed crop production, feed processing, manure management, and background processes (IDF, 2015; Ledgard et al., 2019). Background processes included energy (e.g. electricity, fuel) usage for all processes, including transportation, as well as fertilizer production and crop production. For the feed imported from overseas, the fertilizer, energy use and trans- portation factors for the export countries were used. Animals, brought-in feeds and energy use for the case-study dairy farm are detailed in Table 1, and these data were collected for the 2015/2016 production year.

Based on LCA methodology, NF in the present study referred to all the reactive N (i.e. NH3-N, N20-N, NO3-N and NOX) emissions from the cradle to farm gate involved in the system boundary (Fig. 1). It covered the products of the emission factors of different types of reactive N and the amounts of total N input or energy utilization from all the stages. CF in the present study was total GHGs emissions (including both direct and indirect GHGs) from the cradle to farm gate and was calculated based on the activity factors for each total amount of substance (including fertilizer used during feed production, manure along manure management chain, and all related energy input) and the GHG emission factors. Data sources and parameter conversions are detailed in the following sections.
2.5.2. Functional unit and allocation The functional unit was 1 kg FPCM, which was calculated using the following equation (IDF, 2015):
FPCM = production X (0.1226 X fat% +0.0776 X protein % + 0.2534) (4)

Where, FPCM is the fat- and protein-corrected milk production per year, kg FPCM 1; production is fresh milk production per year, kg.yr-1; fat% is the fat concentration of fresh milk; protein% is the protein concentration of fresh milk.

Emissions were allocated between milk and live-weight sold according to biophysical allocation, after applying economic allo- cation between manure and milk plus live-weight sold, while economic allocation was used between feed co-products (IDF, 2015). The economic value of manure was estimated based on the nutrient value of N and phosphorus (P) left in manure which was assumed to be equivalent to the local prices of mineral fertilizers. The efficiencies of manure N and P as a substitute for mineral fer- tilizer were assumed to be 75% and 90%, respectively (Zhang et al., 2019). In addition, the allocation of total N emissions between the residue and main product for feed production was estimated using economic allocation (IDF, 2015).

25: Based on the four treatments outlined in section 2.3.1, there were four corresponding scenarios in the present study with the same practices for feed crop production, feed processing, and background processes. Details are outlined below.
Baseline: The baseline scenario assumes there is no mitigation measure implemented during manure management, which corre- sponding to the control treatment.
Acid: The acid scenario assumes acidification of the manure surface under slatted floor during housing stage without mitigation measure implemented during storage.
Cover: The cover scenario assumes vermiculite cover on manure
during storage without mitigation measures implemented during housing stage.
Acid & cover: The acid & cover scenario assumes a combination of acidification of the manure surface under slatted floor during housing stage and vermiculite cover on manure during storage.
26: <header>2.5.4. Feeds, animal feed intake and manure management </header>

All feeds used on the farm were produced and processed off- farm. Amounts of feeds used on farm were surveyed (Table 1), and all crop data for site of production and processing were based on published data (Table 3). It was assumed that concentrate feeds used on farm were made from 52% maize grain, 19% soybean meal and 29% wheat bran (Wei, 2016). Electricity used for overall pro- cessing to produce the final concentrate feeds from maize grain, soybean meal and wheat bran was 30 kWh (Bai, 2007; MEPPRC, 2003).

Estimation of N or P excreted by cows was based on the differ- ence between total N or P intake and retention in milk and animal live-weight, which were used for economic allocation of NF and CF between manure and milk plus live-weight sold. Total N or P intake by animals were calculated from the diet composition (Table 1) and average N or P concentration of the feed ingredients (NRC, 2001). N or P retention in milk and animal live-weight were estimated from the nutrient concentrations in milk or live-weight (FAO, 2018).

Manure management is detailed in section 2.2. Volatile solids excreted in manure were calculated based on energy intake by animals and feed digestibility and used for estimating manure CH4 emissions (IPCC, 2006).

(Table 3) and reported emission factors for crop production (Li et al., 2006; Wang et al., 2018a; Wei, 2016; Zhao et al., 2010). Emissions from crop residues were included. In view of local farmer practice, most of the residues were returned to the field during the harvesting. N20 emission from crop residues left in the field was calculated using the emission factor of 1.25% of TN (Rizhiya et al., 2007). It was assumed that all the residues of soybean were burnt in the field in the present study, and NH3, N20 and NOx emissions factors from soybean burning were 13.0, 0.9 and 10.1% of TN, respectively (Andreae and Merlet, 2001; Liu et al., 2011; Shen et al., 2010; Tang et al., 2014). NH3, direct N20 and NO3 emissions from

animal production were from housing and manure storage and the calculations were based on the N retained in manure and emission factors. Emission factors were derived from the incubation trial in the present study. Indirect N2O-N emissions from NH3-N and NO3-N were 0.01 and 0.0075 multiplied by the amount of NH3-N and NO-N emissions, respectively (IPCC, 2006), which were used for the CF calculation but were not used for the NF to avoid double- counting. N emissions from background process, e.g. fertilizer production, transportation, energy production and usage, were estimated using SimaPro 8.5.2.0 and the Ecoinvent database (version 3.3) (Wernet et al., 2016).

GHG emissions in the present study included CH4, CO2, and N20 emissions. Enteric CH4 emission was calculated using gross energy intake and a methane conversion factor (IPCC, 2006). CH4 emis- sions from manure during housing and storage were derived from the incubation trial in the present study. Other emissions, including CH4 and CO2 emissions from background processes, e.g. fertilizer production, energy production and transportation, were calculated using SimaPro 8.5.2.0 and China-specific emission factors. Estima- tion of N20 emissions, both direct and indirect, was detailed in section 2.3.2. The 100-year global warming potential factors for CH4 and N20 relative to CO2 used were 28 and 265, respectively (Stocker et al., 2013).

Changes of NF and CF from the miti- gation techniques included the direct changes of N and GHG emissions originating from the mitigation techniques and the emissions from materials, energy and fuel used for implementation of the mitigation techniques. Usage amount of the materials, en- ergy and fuel has been listed in Table S1, which were from Zhang et al. (2019). Total reactive N losses and GHG emissions from the production, use and transportation of the materials, energy and fuel for each mitigation measures were calculated using SimaPro 8.5.2.0 and the Ecoinvent database (version 3.3) (Wernet et al., 2016).

1: Emissions of NH3, N20 and CH4 from housing under the same treatment showed different temporal patterns (Fig. 2). NH3 emis- sion from the control treatment increased from 64.5 to 118 mg NH3 h in the first 6 h, then decreased to 70 mg NH3 the following 18 h. Acidification of the manure surface almost fully repressed NH3 emission from manure during housing, increasing only from 0.0 to 5.4 mg NH3 m over the 24 h. In contrast, N20 emission from the control treatment was 0.01-0.05 mg N20 m-2 h-¹. In the acidification of manure surface treatment, the variation in N20 emission of 0.01-0.04 mg N20 was similar to that in the control. CH4 emission from the control treatment decreased from 1.2 to 0.7 mg CH4 over the 24 h. CH4 emission in the treatment of acidification of the manure surface showed a small initial decrease. The pH of the manure surface under the control treatment was 8.57-8.72, but decreased to 2.79 immediately after surface acidification and then increased to 7.48 at the end of housing stage (24 h) (Fig. S1).
2: There was only a small effect of acidification of the manure surface, applied at the housing stage, on NH3, N20, and CH4 emis- sions from the subsequent storage stage, whereas the vermiculite cover on manure during storage showed large NH3 emission miti- gation potential (Fig. 3). NH3 emissions from the control dropped rapidly from 657 to 99 mg NH3 during the first 17 days of storage, with a small increase from the 4th day (249 mg NH3 m-2 h-1 to the 10th day (296 mg NH3 h-¹. The variation of NH3 emission during the first 10 days was influenced by the crusting and drying (from day 1-7) as well as shrinking of the crust on the manure surface from day 5-10 (Fig. S2). After the first 17 days, NH3 emission decreased slowly from 99 to 82 mg NH3 m h until the 38th day of the storage. The same pattern of NH3 emission was observed from the acidified manure with a slightly lower value throughout, except initially when it was 36% lower. The vermiculite cover treatment resulted in a significant reduction of NH3 emis- sions to <20 mg NH3 m throughout the storage stage. The N20 emissions from the control treatment increased from 0.00 to 0.54 mg N20 m-2 h-¹. Acidification of the manure surface showed little influence on N20 emission during storage, while the vermic- ulite cover had the greatest effect on N20 emission during storage especially over the last 18 days. CH4 emission from the control

during storage increased from 0.4 to 58 then deceased to 7 mg CH4 Acidification of the manure surface showed little influence on CH4 emissions, whereas the vermiculite cover showed much lower CH4 emissions especially during the first 30 days of storage.

4: The initial manure N excretion onto the housing floor was converted to 100 g manure N equivalent for ease of comparison. N losses under the control treatment were mainly through NH3 emissions, which were 2.8 and 19 g NH3-N during the periods of housing and storage, respectively (Fig. 4). Total N20-N emission during the "housing-storage" period was less than 0.02 g. Apparent other N loss during the "housing-storage" period was 9.1 g based on the measurement of manure N. Under the control treatment, there was 69 g N retained in manure after the storage (i.e. 31% loss). The combination of acidification of the manure surface and the vermiculite cover during storage retained most of the N in manure after storage, which had 90 left (only 10% overall loss; Fig. 4). This coincided with an estimated 0.15 g NH3-N loss, 0.06 g N20-N loss and 9.5 g other N losses during the period of "housing-storage". N retained in manure under the single treatment of acidification of surface manure was least at 73 g, with the largest proportion of N loss as NH3-N (17%) during storage. Apart from the N losses along the "housing-storage" chain, NH4-N, NO-N and organic carbon showed small changes in pool size (Table S2).
5: <header>3.3.1. N and C footprints, as affected by different mitigation measures </header>

The NF and CF of milk were influenced by the NH3 emission mitigation measures (Fig. 5). The baseline NF of the case-study farm was estimated at 6.9 g N kg FPCM. NH3-N emission contributed 4.0 g N kg - FPCM, while the loss of other forms of N totaled 2.9 g N kg-1 FPCM. The mitigation measures reduced the NF of the case- study farm mainly due to the reduction impacts on NH3-N los- ses. Under the three treatments (acidification of manure surface under slatted floor, vermiculite cover during storage, and combi- nation of the two measures), the NF decreased to 6.2, 4.6 and 4.3 g N kg) 1 FPCM, respectively. The CF was also influenced by mitigations, but the effects were negligible. The baseline CF of the case-study farm was 1.00 kg CO2 eq kg FPCM, while the CF of the acidification of manure surface, vermiculite cover and combination treatments were 1.00, 0.99 and 0.98 kg CO2 eq kg- FPCM, respectively.

contributed to most of the N20 and NOx emissions under all sce- narios (>64%). The cover and acid & cover scenarios both led to small increases in the contribution of N loss to water and N20 emission from manure management, while the effects from their implementation (e.g. from production and use of materials, energy and fuel) on NF were minor. CH4 dominated the CF of milk from cradle to farm-gate, with animal enteric fermentation being the main source of CH4 emission (Fig. 7). Total CH4 emission was around 0.50 kg CO2 eq kg FPCM across all treatments, with more than 97% from animal enteric fermentation. The other main contributor to CH4 emission was manure management, at less than 3% of total. While the two treatments with the vermiculite cover decreased manure CH4 emissions (Fig. 3), the effects on total CH4 emission dominated by animal enteric CH4 were negligible (Fig. 7). Feed production was the

largest contributor to CO2 equivalent emission from N20 across all treatments. The differences in total N20 emissions (direct and in- direct) between treatments were small and were mainly from manure management, being least in the combination treatment. Feed production and energy used on farm were the top two con- tributors to CO2 emissions, making up 82% of the total. The CO2 emissions from implementation of mitigations (from energy for production, transport and use of acid and vermiculite) were <0.5% of total CO2 emissions.

2011; Oenema et al., 2014). Previous studies have shown that acidification of manure has a large NH3 emission mitigation po- tential from the manure management system (e.g. Fangueiro et al., 2015). Studies with dairy slurry revealed that acidification of slurry to pH 4.5-5.5 could reduce NH3 emission by 81-95%, with negli- gible NH3 emission at pH 3.5 (Petersen et al., 2012; Regueiro et al., 2016). Neerackal et al. (2017) showed that sprinkling low-pH water on manure between manure flushing cycles resulted in a nearly 87% reduction in NH3 emission, while flushing with low-pH water gave a 70% reduction in NH3 emissions. Other acidic substance additives could also reduce NH3 emission from manure. It was reported that applying sodium bisulfate to dairy slurry reduced total NH3 emis- sions by 85% and 60% on the first day and first three days, respec- tively (Sun et al., 2008). Thus, different methods of acidification have all shown large NH3 mitigation impacts. Evidence from this study suggested that acidification of the manure surface under a slatted floor could reduce NH3 emission over one-day by 98%, which was higher than the results observed from previous studies for manure storage (Petersen et al., 2012; Regueiro et al., 2016). In the present study, the acid was applied to the surface of the manure. This lowered the pH of the manure surface thereby reducing total NH3 emissions (Fig. 2). In addition, the acid on the manure surface might form a cover and decrease the NH3 con- centration on the surface, which could further reduce NH3 emission from manure. Compared to other mitigation measures (e.g. diet manipulation), acidified slurry could improve the availability of N after it is applied to soil as fertilizer (Fangueiro et al., 2015). We did not simulate manure dropping down from the slatted floor during the trial, and the related disturbance on the diluted H2SO4 layer by continuous addition of fresh manure could reduce NH3 mitigation efficiency. This effect could not be examined in this study.

Most of the previous studies haven't examined the impacts of the mitigation measures on other gaseous emissions, nor the NH3 emission from the downstream stages. Kai et al. (2008) examined the impacts of acidification on NH3 emission from downstream storage and land application, and showed that NH3 emission could be reduced by 90 and 67%, respectively. In the present study, acidification of surface manure reduced NH3 emission during storage by 17%. The lower NH3 reduction might have been due to the lower degree of acidification of the total manure which was fully mixed before going into the storage stage. In contrast to other studies where acid was mixed with the manure (Kai et al., 2008; Petersen et al., 2012; Regueiro et al., 2016), acidification of the manure surface in the present study could reduce initial NH3 emission from the manure surface with a lower cost due to lower amount required and less negative acidification effects on soil after it is applied. The impacts of mitigation measures on other gaseous emissions were also examined in the present study, which allowed a more comprehensive analysis of the mitigation measures.

VanderZaag et al. (2008) summarized previous studies using different cover materials on manure and revealed that when they were installed properly they could reduce NH3 emission by more than 70%. Consistent with expectations (VanderZaag et al., 2008), the vermiculite cover used in this study reduced NH3 emission during storage by over 97% (Fig. 3). In addition, as vermiculite has been used as economic and nature-friendly amendments, manure mixed with vermiculite used during storage as organic fertilizer could further improve N availability (Mazloomi and Jalali, 2019). Reduction of NH3 emission was due to limitation of air flow and the adsorption capacity for ammonium of vermiculite (Brady and Weil, 2002; Wang et al., 2011). CH4 emission also decreased with the vermiculite cover, possibly due to more oxygen existing in the contact layer of manure and vermiculite, which reduced the anaerobic environment and restricted CH4 production and emis- sion (Vor et al., 2003). Moreover, vermiculite might have adsorbed

CH4 since montmorillonite with a similar structure has been veri- fied to have CH4-adsorbing properties (Ji et al., 2012). The vermic- ulite cover increased N20 emission (Fig. 3). This was because of more oxygen occurring in the mixture of manure and vermiculite, thereby accelerating the nitrification of NH4, and providing more substrates for denitrification and emission of more N20 from the bulk manure (Sommer et al., 2006).

Compared to other mitigation measures (e.g. diet manipulation), acidified slurry could improve the availability of N after it is applied to soil as fertilizer (Fangueiro et al., 2015). We did not simulate manure dropping down from the slatted floor during the trial, and the related disturbance on the diluted H2SO4 layer by continuous addition of fresh manure could reduce NH3 mitigation efficiency. This effect could not be examined in this study.

Most of the previous studies haven't examined the impacts of the mitigation measures on other gaseous emissions, nor the NH3 emission from the downstream stages. Kai et al. (2008) examined the impacts of acidification on NH3 emission from downstream storage and land application, and showed that NH3 emission could be reduced by 90 and 67%, respectively. VanderZaag et al. (2008) summarized previous studies using different cover materials on manure and revealed that when they were installed properly they could reduce NH3 emission by more than 70%. Reduction of NH3 emission was due to limitation of air flow and the adsorption capacity for ammonium of vermiculite (Brady and Weil, 2002; Wang et al., 2011). Moreover, vermiculite might have adsorbed

CH4 since montmorillonite with a similar structure has been veri- fied to have CH4-adsorbing properties (Ji et al., 2012). The vermic- ulite cover increased N20 emission (Fig. 3). This was because of more oxygen occurring in the mixture of manure and vermiculite, thereby accelerating the nitrification of NH4, and providing more substrates for denitrification and emission of more N20 from the bulk manure (Sommer et al., 2006).

16: <header>4.2. N and C footprints based on emission factors derived from different sources </header>

As well as assessing impacts on direct NH3, N20, and CH4 emissions, the present holistic analysis also considered the influ- ence of the mitigation measures on the reactive NF and CF of milk from cradle-to-farm-gate. This was based on using LCA methodol- ogy that recognized manure as residue (i.e. all emissions to the point of leaving the farm are assigned to milk and live-weight sold for meat, while subsequent emissions following land application are assigned to the receiving crop; FAO, 2018). A consequence of an effective mitigation is that a greater N content is left in manure, which could increase its fertilizer value and benefit crop growth but with an increased potential size of NH3 emission after spreading or application (Webb et al., 2005). To give an unbiased assessment, we also analyzed the influence of an alternative method of LCA allo- cation between milk, meat and manure on the estimated footprint of milk. If an economic allocation method has been used that put an economic value (or fertilizer-equivalent value) of the "sold" manure, it would have resulted in an average of 0.5% of total emissions assigned to manure, thereby marginally reducing the emissions allocated to milk (and live-weight sold for meat). Thus, the residue or economic allocation methods showed little impact of the manure value on the calculated reactive NF and CF.

The NF of 7-9 N kg-1 milk from two housed USA dairy farm systems estimated by Powell and Rotz (2015) was close to the re- sults in the present study (4-7 kg N kg FPCM). In contrast, Ledgard et al. (2019) estimated the NF of milk from survey farms in the same region of China at 11.9 g N kg FPCM, which is about twice that from the present study. This difference was largely due to the different emission factors used in the two studies, which directly influenced the estimates of N emissions. In the present study, NH3 and N20 emissions from manure management were based on the results from the present incubation trial, whereas Ledgard et al. (2019) used factors from previous studies by Wei (2016). In addition, N losses from Ledgard et al. (2019) included all the emissions from housing, storage and subsequent treatment (e.g. composting and anaerobic digestion), while N losses from the manure treatment were not included in the present study. In the present study, emission factors from feed production were directly derived from previous studies without considering the climatic conditions and soil properties, which might cause some un- certainties in NF and CF. However, as the same dataset was used for feed production and processing sectors, impacts on calculated NF or CF for the base system and the selected mitigation measures would be same.

In the current study, the estimates of NF for the baseline case- study farm, with or without mitigation measures, were based on the same parameter dataset and therefore the influence of miti- gation measures on the NF could be directly compared. The reduction of NF was achieved mainly by the decrease of NH3 emissions, with relatively little change in loss of other forms of N from manure management and negligible background N losses from implementation of mitigation techniques (Figs. 5 and 6). The CF value estimated in the present study was within the range of 0.6-2.1 kg CO2eq kg-1 FPCM summarized in a review of the CF of milk including 87 values from 30 published LCAs of dairy

production (Lorenz et al., 2019). In addition, the CF of milk from studies in the same region of China was estimated to be 1.08-1.34 kg CO2 eq kg` FPCM (Ledgard et al., 2019; Wang et al., 2018a), which is similar to the baseline value of 1.0 kg CO2 eq kg- FPCM found in the present study. A higher CF of milk esti- mated by Wang et al. (2018a) was partly due to a higher allocation factor to milk (0.95 compared to 0.85 in the present study), which was based on using an economic allocation method. There was a small reduction of CF of milk using all three mitigation measures at 1-3%. This reduction was much less than the corresponding 10-38% decrease in the NF of milk.

From a life cycle perspective, mitigation measures have shown great NH3 mitigation potential with more N retained in manure, resulting in higher N loss potential downstream along the manure management chain (e.g. after application), which was not included in the system boundary in the present study. In normal practice, most of the manure will be applied on orchards or vegetable gar- dens, which should be included in the orchard or vegetable pro- duction system. Furthermore, in North China Plain, livestock production and feed production were not linked directly with each other. The present study mainly considered the mitigation effects on milk production, while application of manure with more effec- tive N could supply more nutrient for crop which will also benefit the fertilizer replacement. However, this is another complex topic, which could be further analyzed in future.

21: Our results based on the incubation trials may not reflect the real environmental conditions but show the potential influence of the mitigation measures on NH3 and the other GHG emissions from consecutive housing and storage stages. Acidification of manure and a vermiculite cover showed large NH3 mitigation potential when emissions from both housing and storage stages were accounted for. The holistic life cycle analysis, including analysis of possible trade-off of losses in different N forms, from various sources or from the production and use of mitigation materials for the mitigation measures, showed a decrease in the NF of milk of up to 38% with a smaller reduction in CF of milk by 1-3%. Adoption of these measures might be a solution to alleviate the negative effects on air and water quality without raising the risk of global warming. However, holistic farm-scale trials should be conducted to achieve more robust quantitative data of gaseous emissions from animal housing and manure management.
